evidence: Multiple modules define local mirror settings types and snapshot adapters instead of reusing shared config value objects (e.g., startuporchestrator defines startupUpdateSettings/startupStartramSettings/startupSwapSettings and importer defines importerSwapSettings/importerStartramSettings, each mapped from config snapshots).

Runtime dependency surfaces are very wide and mixed-concern: docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go exposes many callback bags, startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go defines large startup/background runtime callback structs, and importer/importer.go embeds broad orchestration ops plus importer-specific callbacks.

Several call paths add delegate-only layers with repeated runtime resolution and no additional policy, especially in shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go (persistShipUrbit* family) and main.go/bootstrapRuntime method wrappers.

Section-scoped config mutation is implemented as many parallel wrappers in multiple layers (`UpdateUrbitRuntimeConfig`, `UpdateUrbitNetworkConfig`, etc. in `goseg/config/urbit.go:133-188`; mirrored callback fields in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:66-77`; similar per-section mutators in `goseg/structs/configs.go:110-163`).

Urbit workflow runtime file mixes distinct domains (transition dispatch, alias validation, docker lifecycle, schedule persistence, MinIO link lifecycle) in one module (`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go:19-466`).

Several test files accumulate unrelated behavior families and repeated seam setup/command scaffolding (`goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go`, `goseg/system/wifi_test.go`, `goseg/importer/importer_test.go`), increasing maintenance friction and making intent harder to isolate.

goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go:164 discards `resolveWorkflowRuntime` error (`resolvedRuntime, _ := ...`) before emitting transitions, while sibling path `dispatchUploadImport` in the same file returns runtime-resolution errors.
goseg/broadcast/loop.go:143 ignores `runBroadcastTickWithRuntime` return value completely, but the looped path in the same file conditionally routes tick errors via `tickErrorFn`.

goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:176 emits `network reachability check failed` without underlying cause, and the injected callback type used by startup/main is `func(string) bool`, preventing causal error propagation at this boundary.

goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_health.go:100-104 logs recovery errors and clears strike state, while `subsystem_events.go:149` escalates repeated transition errors to a returned error; subsystem failure handling policy is inconsistent.

findings.summary: Feature packages duplicate config DTOs and adapter functions, creating low-leverage indirection with drift risk.
evidence: startuporchestrator defines startupUpdateSettings/startupStartramSettings/startupSwapSettings and maps each from config snapshots via startup*Snapshot functions.
importer defines importerSwapSettings/importerStartramSettings and maps from config.SwapSettingsSnapshot/config.StartramSettingsSnapshot in swapSettingsSnapshot/startramSettingsSnapshot.
subsystem_health copies Check502Settings into another struct literal immediately after reading a snapshot, reinforcing repeated DTO translation layers.
config/config.go already defines canonical settings structs (UpdateSettings, StartramSettings, SwapSettings, Check502Settings), so package-local mirrors are mostly pass-through adapters.
findings.suggestion: Introduce shared read models for startup/importer/subsystem consumers (or narrow typed views in config) and remove package-local mirror structs where fields are 1:1. Keep only true anti-corruption mappings where schema differs materially.

findings.summary: Large callback-bag runtimes combine unrelated responsibilities, increasing indirection cost and test setup burden.
evidence: runtime_ops.go defines many broad runtime structs (RuntimeContainerOps, RuntimeUrbitOps, RuntimeStartupOps, etc.) with large callback surfaces rather than smaller task interfaces.
startup_orchestrator.go uses a very wide startBackgroundServicesRuntime with many callback fields spanning unrelated subsystems.

importer.go’s importerProvisionRuntime embeds orchestration RuntimeContainerOps/RuntimeVolumeOps plus many importer-specific callbacks, mixing orchestration, shipworkflow, filesystem, and transition concerns.
startup_orchestrator_test.go requires helper switch machinery (withStartBackgroundServiceCallback) to set many callbacks, indicating high ceremony to exercise focused behavior.
findings.suggestion: Split callback bags by use-case (e.g., startup-init core/host/storage service interfaces, importer provisioning phases) and inject only the minimal interfaces each function needs. This reduces hidden coupling and test scaffolding.

findings.summary: Repeated resolve-and-delegate wrappers add call depth without adding policy or translation.
evidence: shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go has multiple persistShipUrbit* functions that all perform identical resolveWorkflowRuntime error handling then one direct callback call.
main.go’s bootstrapRuntime methods (bootstrap/startServer/runC2cCheck) mainly duplicate nil checks and delegate to stored callbacks, while higher-level callers already validate runtime.
wifi_runtime_api.go methods repeatedly call prepareForUse for guard logic before single runtime calls, leading to similar boilerplate across methods.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate resolve/guard logic into shared execution helpers (e.g., withResolvedWorkflowRuntime, withPreparedWiFiRuntime) and remove one-line delegates unless they encode distinct policy. Keep exported APIs thin but behaviorally meaningful.

findings.summary: Section-specific config mutation is duplicated across structs, config persistence, and runtime seam layers, creating shotgun-change pressure.
evidence: `goseg/config/urbit.go:133-188` defines six nearly identical section mutators with repeated nil-guard and forwarding logic.
`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:66-77` exposes a parallel set of six callback fields for the same urbit sections.
`goseg/structs/configs.go:110-163` repeats per-section update wrappers for system config with the same guard-and-mutate pattern.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single section-update abstraction (typed section registry or generic section updater) and have runtime seams expose one cohesive mutator API instead of per-section callback fan-out.

findings.summary: Urbit operations runtime module blends multiple responsibilities that should be separated by concern boundaries.
evidence: `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` combines transition routing (`runUrbitTransitionFromCommand`), alias/domain orchestration (`buildUrbitDomainSteps`, `buildMinIODomainSteps`), docker lifecycle control (`buildRebuildContainerSteps`, `buildRuntimeConfigIntSteps`), and scheduling updates (`schedulePack`, `pausePackSchedule`) in one file.
The same file also owns MinIO link orchestration state-machine logic (`minIOLinkTransitionCoordinator` and related helpers), adding another domain slice into the same unit.
This mixed composition directly depends on config persistence callbacks and container controls from separate subsystems.
findings.suggestion: Split the file into focused modules (e.g., domain-alias transitions, runtime-config transitions, MinIO-link transitions, scheduling transitions) and keep each module limited to one operational axis with a narrower dependency surface.

findings.summary: Large test files aggregate unrelated behavior domains and repeated setup idioms, reducing coherence and increasing coupling between test scenarios.
evidence: `goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go` covers config generation, parameter validation, process probing, startup/teardown shell orchestration, and detailed error-path matrices in one file (943 lines).
`goseg/system/wifi_test.go` combines low-level nmcli parsing, wifi client behavior, C2C message routing, service construction, and runtime loop lifecycle tests in one file (650 lines).
`goseg/importer/importer_test.go` mixes HTTP validation, chunk persistence/combine, transition broadcasting, workflow dispatch, and environment provisioning scenarios in one file (898 lines).
`goseg/main_test.go` combines middleware/file-serving tests, connectivity-mode logic, bootstrap wiring, and server lifecycle behavior in one file.
findings.suggestion: Split by behavior slice (API validation, command orchestration, state lifecycle, integration-path tests) and extract shared seam builders/fixtures into dedicated test helper packages to eliminate repeated setup scaffolding.

findings.summary: Router command-flow tests duplicate long command/error matrices rather than using reusable table-driven helpers.
evidence: `goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go:606-708` and `:754-843` each define extensive failure matrices with repeated command capture, injected shell behavior, and similar assertion shape.
`goseg/system/wifi_test.go` repeats comparable command call-capture/assert patterns across multiple tests (`TestIfCheckAndToggleDeviceUseSeams`, `TestConnectToWifiUsesNmcliAndPropagatesErrors`).
The same pattern appears with bespoke per-test setup rather than a single reusable scenario runner.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared command-scenario harness (runtime builder + expected command/assert helper) and drive these matrices from data tables to reduce structural repetition and improve intent readability.

findings.summary: Protocol action and contract tests overlap heavily in lifecycle-metadata verification, causing duplicated policy checks across files.
evidence: `goseg/protocol/actions/actions_test.go` hard-codes lifecycle metadata expectations and compatibility assertions (including fixed introduced versions and contract parity checks).
`goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts_test.go` already validates lifecycle metadata integrity and binding/catalog consistency for the same contract space.
Both files assert similar invariants from different entry points, so metadata evolution requires touching multiple overlapping suites.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate lifecycle policy assertions into a shared contract-validation helper driven by catalog entries, and keep action tests focused on action-specific behavior (parsing/dispatch) rather than catalog invariants.

findings.summary: Session modules blend token bookkeeping, connection lifecycle mutation, cleanup policy, and transport-specific storage without a common abstraction boundary.
evidence: `goseg/session/connection_registry.go` handles token registration/removal, auth/unauth bucket transitions, stale cleanup, and connection lookup/removal in one type.
`goseg/session/logstream_session_store.go` combines system-log session sets, docker log session maps, pending-removal queues, and message bus management in one store.
Both files contain repeated map/slice copy/filter/remove patterns implemented independently.
findings.suggestion: Introduce focused components (token index, connection bucket manager, logstream subscription store) with shared collection helpers so lifecycle policy is centralized and transport-specific concerns stay isolated.

findings.summary: Runtime validation failures are silently dropped in transition paths, creating hidden misconfiguration failures.
evidence: operations_runtime.go:164 ignores `resolveWorkflowRuntime` error with `_`, then continues with potentially zero-value runtime for transition emission.
operations_runtime.go:181-184 in the same module falls back to default runtime on resolution error, again without surfacing the validation failure to callers.
broadcast/loop.go:143 drops tick errors (`_ = runBroadcastTickWithRuntime(...)`) while the looping path at 131-135 can forward errors through `tickErrorFn`.
findings.suggestion: Standardize runtime-resolution behavior: return or wrap runtime validation errors from both transition emission and tick entrypoints, and only use best-effort mode when explicitly selected by policy. Add a shared helper that requires an explicit `critical|non_critical` error policy at each callsite.

findings.summary: Network reachability failures lose root-cause context due to boolean callback contracts and generic error messages.
evidence: startup_orchestrator.go:173-176 uses `NetworkReachabilityFn(cloudCheckHost) bool` and returns a generic `network reachability check failed` error with no causal detail.
main.go:159 and startup_orchestrator.go:224 both model reachability as `func(string) bool`, so upstream layers cannot wrap DNS/socket/timeouts with `%w`.
findings.suggestion: Change reachability seams to `func(string) error` (or `(bool, error)`), then wrap failures with operation/context (`fmt.Errorf("network reachability %s: %w", host, err)`) so startup callers can distinguish transient network failures from runtime misconfiguration.

findings.summary: Recovered panics are flattened into string errors without stack/type context, reducing failure diagnosability compared to normal wrapped errors.
evidence: startup_orchestrator.go:675 converts panic payload via `%v` into `error` text (`background service panic: %v`) and drops stack trace and panic type.
In the same startup path, normal failures are wrapped with `%w` at 679, so panic failures and non-panic failures produce different fidelity and comparability.
subsystem_events.go uses returned wrapped errors (e.g., `docker event error: %w` at 114), highlighting inconsistent diagnostic depth across long-running orchestration loops.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared panic-to-error adapter that captures stack (`debug.Stack()`), preserves panic type/value, and returns a structured wrapped error type so orchestration supervisors can handle panic and non-panic failures uniformly.

findings.summary: Docker subsystem loops apply conflicting escalation policies for repeated failures, causing unpredictable operator-facing failure modes.
evidence: subsystem_health.go:100-104 logs wireguard recovery failure and always clears the strike counter (`delete(status, pier)`), effectively suppressing repeated failure escalation for that path.
subsystem_events.go:145-150 increments failure count and returns hard error after threshold, terminating subsystem path on repeated failures.
Both are long-running docker subsystem loops, but one is fail-open and one is fail-fast without a shared policy contract.
findings.suggestion: Define a unified subsystem error policy (max retries/window + escalation target) and apply it to both health and event loops. Keep counters when recovery fails, and emit structured escalation errors once threshold is exceeded.
